title: Useful Fictions or Distorting Frames? How Models Shape Drug R&D - TimmermanReport.com
gist: Analytical piece (via David Shaywitz, drawing on a Decibio interview with Jack Scannell) arguing that biopharma R&D productivity decline (Eroom's Law) and AI's promise are both bottlenecked by the predictive validity of the biological and financial models the industry relies on, not by compute or throughput; reductionist target-first drug discovery often misses polypharmacological 'magic shotgun' drugs, and applying AI to poor biological models just scales wrong answers faster.

themes.theme: Eroom's Law and R&D productivity decline

insights.claim: Jack Scannell coined 'Eroom's Law' (Moore spelled backward) to describe how, from 1950 to roughly 2010, the number of new drugs approved per inflation-adjusted billion dollars of R&D spending fell by about half every nine years, the inverse of computing's Moore's Law.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: A quantified base rate for structural decline in pharma R&D returns useful for evaluating whether new platform technologies (AI, better models) can actually bend the productivity curve.
insights.quote: from 1950 to roughly 2010, the number of new drugs approved per inflation-adjusted billion dollars of R&D spending fell by about half every nine years

insights.claim: Jack Scannell argues that standard drug and biotech valuation models are 'blind to decision quality,' which makes companies less sensitive to the value of making better decisions, especially early in development.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Investors relying on conventional biotech valuation frameworks may be systematically underpricing the value of superior early-stage decision-making processes at R&D-stage companies.
insights.quote: standard drug and biotech valuation models are blind to decision quality
insights.locator: paragraph following the Eroom's Law introduction

themes.theme: Reductionism's blind spots in drug discovery

insights.claim: High-throughput assays that select for the most potent single-target binders can generate premature confidence in a drug candidate, because tight binding to an individual target does not reliably predict whether a compound will work in the whole organism.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: In diligence on platform biotechs that tout potent single-target binders, investors should ask whether phenotypic or in-organism validation backs the target-binding data before assuming translational promise.
insights.quote: identifying a great ligand is not the same as finding a promising drug candidate

insights.claim: Some of the most effective drugs behave as 'magic shotguns' hitting multiple targets rather than 'magic bullets' precisely tuned to one target; clozapine, the only drug approved specifically for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, binds dopamine, serotonin, histamine, muscarinic and adrenergic receptors, and after more than 50 years its mechanism of action remains unclear, with efficacy possibly depending on this multi-receptor promiscuity.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Platform companies chasing hyper-selective single-target drugs for complex CNS/psychiatric disease may be optimizing the wrong metric; polypharmacology can be a feature, not a bug, in some indications.
insights.locator: When Clean Stories Meet Messy Biology

insights.claim: Some tyrosine kinase inhibitors developed against a single specified kinase target turned out to inhibit broad panels of kinases, and their clinical activity may depend on this off-target breadth rather than the originally intended single-target mechanism.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Mechanism-of-action narratives used to justify oncology drug valuations may be incomplete; broader off-target kinase activity could be doing the clinical work, a nuance worth probing in diligence.
insights.locator: When Clean Stories Meet Messy Biology, TKI sentence

insights.claim: Several important drugs approved before the target-centric era of drug discovery -metformin, the first antipsychotics such as chlorpromazine, and the first antidepressants- have messy or contested mechanisms and were discovered empirically, with mechanistic stories constructed only after the fact.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: A clean, well-articulated mechanism-of-action narrative is not a prerequisite for clinical and commercial success; its absence shouldn't automatically be treated as a red flag, especially for empirically-derived legacy drug classes.
insights.quote: Metformin has been attributed to AMPK activation, mitochondrial complex I inhibition, and effects on gut microbiota, with little consensus around a single unifying mechanism.
insights.locator: When Clean Stories Meet Messy Biology, metformin paragraph

insights.claim: Anti-VEGF therapy's original rationale -Judah Folkman's thesis that tumors depend on angiogenesis- proved incomplete: subsequent research by Rakesh Jain suggested anti-VEGF agents also transiently normalize chaotic tumor vasculature, improving chemotherapy delivery, showing that even well-validated oncology mechanism stories keep getting more complicated as more data accumulates.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Even 'settled' mechanism-of-action stories behind blockbuster drug classes can evolve; treat MoA narratives as provisional inputs to valuation, not fixed facts.
insights.locator: When Clean Stories Meet Messy Biology, Anti-VEGF paragraph

themes.theme: Predictive validity of disease models and AI

insights.claim: Jack Scannell argues that marginally better disease/biological models are disproportionately more valuable than marginally worse ones -a small improvement in a model's predictive validity can outweigh running many more compounds through a less predictive system.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: When evaluating biotech platforms, weight investment in model fidelity/validation over raw screening throughput or compound volume.
insights.quote: marginally better models are much better than marginally worse models
insights.locator: A Little More Predictive Validity Goes a Long Way

insights.claim: Feeding AI-driven drug discovery with data from poor biological models 'simply increases the number of wrong answers you can generate per second'; some AI drug-development companies may industrialize a poor biological model because it is easiest to fit into an AI-led design-make-test loop, producing virtual ligands optimized against an irrelevant in-silico system.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: A strong caution against valuing AI-native drug discovery startups purely on the speed/throughput of their design-make-test loop; the underlying biological model's fidelity is the binding constraint, and AI scales errors as fast as insights.
insights.quote: feeding AI with data from poor biological models simply increases the number of wrong answers you can generate per second
insights.locator: A Little More Predictive Validity Goes a Long Way, closing paragraph

insights.claim: Some AI drug-development companies are instead focused on using AI to build better underlying biological models, rather than just accelerating design-make-test cycles on existing, possibly flawed, models.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Differentiates AI-biotech investment targets: favor companies building/improving the underlying biological model over those bolting AI onto legacy assay pipelines.
insights.locator: A Little More Predictive Validity Goes a Long Way, final sentence

themes.theme: Incentive structures that keep bad models alive

insights.claim: Academic researchers are not rewarded for the slow, unglamorous work of validating whether disease models (e.g., mouse models) actually recapitulate human pathology and drug response, so validation work is systematically underfunded even though everyone agrees better models are needed.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Model-validation science is a structurally underinvested public good in biomedical research -a potential niche for philanthropic or specialized venture funding to fill a genuine market gap.
insights.quote: do not get Nature papers by doing long, tedious, gritty work to evaluate whether a particular mouse model of disease X recapitulates the human pathology

insights.claim: Industry researchers want better disease models but lack incentive to build them because a validated model quickly becomes useful to competitors too, eroding any competitive advantage, and privately acknowledging a model's limitations can threaten the internal programs built on it.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Explains why proprietary model-validation breakthroughs are rare in industry; a company credibly claiming a validated, non-commoditizable model may hold a real moat worth scrutinizing in diligence.
insights.locator: Why Flawed Models Endure, industry researchers paragraph

insights.claim: At the portfolio-management level within pharma, simple explanatory narratives (e.g., 'three or four boxes and a couple of arrows') systematically win out over more nuanced, complex biological models, because portfolio committees and boards will not approve projects presented with complex pathway maps.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Internal pharma capital-allocation decisions may be biased toward legible, simplified narratives over more accurate but complex science -a structural inefficiency that can create mispriced opportunities for outsiders willing to engage with complexity.
insights.quote: three or four boxes and a couple of arrows
insights.locator: Why Flawed Models Endure, portfolio committee anecdote

themes.theme: AI in drug R&D as a sociotechnical problem

insights.claim: Jack Scannell, applying a framework from innovation scholar Fred Steward, distinguishes 'technoscientific' problems solvable with enough engineers and funding (like the Apollo moonshot) from 'sociotechnical' problems requiring simultaneous economic, regulatory, and process change over time (like building modern healthcare systems), and argues AI in drug R&D likely belongs in the harder, sociotechnical category.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for near-term AI-driven productivity gains in biopharma; model AI-in-R&D returns on a multi-year sociotechnical adoption curve rather than a moonshot/engineering timeline.
insights.quote: a complicated mix of economic, regulatory, and process changes happening simultaneously over time

insights.claim: Drawing on Paul David's research on factory electrification, the piece notes electricity itself did not immediately boost factory productivity -gains materialized only once factory work was reorganized around the new technology- suggesting AI in drug R&D may similarly show delayed, structurally-contingent productivity gains rather than immediate ROI.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations against near-term ROI claims from AI-in-pharma vendors and platform companies; the payoff is more likely tied to later workflow/organizational redesign than to the technology rollout itself.

insights.claim: Jack Scannell states that 'no one really knows what the reconfigured factory is going to look like when it comes to AI in drug R&D,' an explicit acknowledgment of genuine uncertainty about the eventual organizational structure of AI-enabled biopharma R&D.
insights.speaker: Timmerman Report
insights.so_what: Current AI-in-pharma organizational models -whether fully AI-native biotechs or AI-augmented legacy pharma workflows- remain experimental, not proven playbooks investors can pattern-match against.
insights.quote: I suspect that no one really knows what the reconfigured factory is going to look like when it comes to AI in drug R&D
